text: The surgeon applies gentle traction using the robotic fourth arm during the posterior dissection. Scissors and a bipolar instrument are used to manipulate the tissue, with the tip of the Foley catheter visible in the surgical field.
The surgeon identifies the rectum as a reliable anatomical landmark, noting the yellow fat surrounding it and the fold where the peritoneum reflects off the rectum and ascends towards the bladder.

The surgeon lifts the tissue slightly to reveal a fold, then makes a small perineotomy approximately half a centimeter above the fold. This exposes the seminal vesicles and vas deferens while ensuring the ureters are not disturbed by staying low and midline.

The surgeon uses blunt dissection with the left hand positioned up and the right hand down, navigating into the correct anatomical plane. As the dissection progresses, the seminal vesicles and vas deferens are revealed, located lower than initially anticipated. The vas deferens is identified on the right-hand side, facilitated by the blunt dissection technique.

The surgeon identifies the vas deferens by tracing the white structure to the midline, ensuring it is not the ureter. Two white structures meet at the midline, confirming the vas deferens. Directly behind the vas deferens, a grayish structure is visible, identified as the seminal vesicle.

The surgeon confirms the identification of the vas deferens and seminal vesicle, distinguishing them from the ureter. The vas deferens and seminal vesicle are clearly visible, and the surgeon prepares to dissect them out carefully.

The surgeon uses cautery directly on the seminal vesicle, ensuring localized heat application without affecting surrounding tissues. The seminal vesicle is dissected off using blunt dissection, following natural tissue planes, without the need for clips. The procedure is repeated on the left side.

The surgeon uses cautery to spot treat areas around the left seminal vesicle and vas deferens, minimizing thermal spread by reducing pedal time. The assistant provides exposure by lifting tissue during the posterior dissection.

The surgeon uses both hands to dissect directly on the seminal vesicles and vas deferens, emphasizing the importance of avoiding dissection around these structures to prevent injury to surrounding tissues. The approach is carefully chosen based on the patient's surgical history.

The surgeon makes a small incision to open the non-vas tissue slightly, allowing the seminal vesicles and vas deferens to be lifted through the bladder neck without adhering to the rectum. The dissection reveals the point where the two vas deferens converge, marking the location of the bladder neck.

The surgeon dissects up to the point where the two vas deferens meet, ensuring clear identification of the bladder neck. The approach avoids dissecting through adhesions in cases where the bowel is stuck to the posterior bladder, opting instead for an anterior approach through the bladder neck. The dissection begins lateral to the lateral umbilical ligament to establish the lower limit, particularly in patients with prior mesh hernia repairs or obesity.

The surgeon begins dissection on the right-hand side, working through areolar tissue without reaching the pubic bone, then proceeds to the left-hand side, dissecting lateral to the lateral umbilical ligament down to the vas deferens. The dissection is kept shallow to maintain orientation, and once both sides are dissected, the surgeon connects the dissection across the top, avoiding the bladder.

The surgeon continues dissection in the thin areolar tissue, aligning with the previously defined lateral dissection boundaries. Using a zero-degree lens, the surgeon performs posterior dissection of the seminal vesicles and lifts the prostate gland to expose the posterior bladder neck.

The surgeon dissects the fat from the pelvis to expose the endopelvic fascia and the junction between the bladder and prostate. The fat is more adherent at this junction, and the surgeon cauterizes the superficial dorsal vein located deep to this tissue before continuing laterally to remove all the fat.

The surgeon opens the endopelvic fascia using cautery, causing the underlying muscle to contract and create a space, facilitating the dissection. The technique helps avoid injury to the Santorini plexus and potential bleeding. The surgeon also considers the presence of an accessory pudendal artery during the procedure.

The surgeon identifies and manages a small accessory vessel using bipolar cautery, ensuring minimal dissection around the dorsal vein. The surgeon carefully applies cautery to blanch the muscle and maintain dissection away from the levator muscle, minimizing thermal damage. The surgical field is in thin areolar tissue, and any small bleeders are addressed with selective bipolar cautery.

The surgeon prepares to use two needle drivers as the fourth arm becomes crucial for dynamic manipulation after passing through the anterior bladder neck. The surgeon ties off the dorsal vein to prevent back bleeding, ensuring thorough dissection around the dorsal vein complex and levators before placing a single stitch.

The surgeon uses the left hand to apply tension on the bladder while the open scissor jaws are positioned in the correct plane, causing the bladder muscle to contract and spring back, aiding in the dissection.

The surgeon gently grazes the tissue with an open scissor jaw, revealing the urethra as it approaches the prostate gland from the bladder. The muscle melts away in the correct plane, while darker areas indicate where the jaws were closed. The surgeon aims to preserve the bladder neck tissue unless aggressive cancer is present.

The surgeon observes the longitudinal fibers of the urethra as it enters the prostate gland, with the bladder positioned posteriorly. The urethra is dissected from nine to three o'clock, revealing its thickness. The fourth arm is utilized to assist in grabbing the Foley catheter, while the assistant provides counter-traction. The posterior bladder neck is exposed using a spreading technique with the left hand.

The surgeon cuts through the muscle of the bladder neck, maintaining traction to expose a lip of prostate tissue. This maneuver lifts the prostate significantly out of the pelvis, enhancing visibility of the tissue planes. The surgeon performs an 'up and out' move to improve exposure and create tension, revealing a clear line indicating the plane. The bladder is checked to ensure its orientation is straight down, indicating further dissection is needed.

The surgeon carefully inspects the bladder to avoid buttonholing, then traces the median lobe if present. The dissection reveals longitudinal fibers indicating proximity to the seminal vesicles and vas deferens. The surgeon opens the area further, preparing for posterior reconstruction by targeting these fibers for attachment to the recta urethralis.

The surgeon carefully thins out tissue at the corners away from the neurovascular bundles, identifying a small vessel in the process. Sherry assists by dropping in probes for nerve mapping, inserting monopolar listening electrodes into the distal part of the levator complex near the urethra on both sides.

The surgeon switches to a round-tip scissor for nerve-sparing, starting the dissection near the posterior lip of the prostate gland. The goal is to reach Santorini's plexus, identifying the correct plane for nerve preservation.

The surgeon uses a bipolar instrument to stimulate the perineal branch of the pudendal nerve, causing visible contraction of the pelvic floor muscles. The bipolar instrument's jaws are adjusted to target either a wide or narrow area for stimulation. The nerve and muscle are visually distinguishable, with the muscle twitching upon nerve stimulation.

The surgeon opens the superficial layer near the prostate gland, revealing the nerve responsible for urinary control. The nerve is visually identified as being slightly pulled off the prostate, situated within the pelvic floor area. The surgeon emphasizes the importance of preserving this nerve throughout the dissection to ensure better urinary control recovery.

The surgeon demonstrates the contraction of the pelvic floor muscles, highlighting the perineal branch of the pudendal nerve sitting on top of the prostate gland. As the superficial layer is opened, the nerve is carefully dissected away from the prostate, aiding in nerve sparing for both sexual function and urinary control.

The surgeon opens the superficial layer of the lateral prostatic fascia, revealing the Santorini plexus and creating a separation between the nerve and the prostate gland. The nerve is visibly pushed off to the side, demonstrating the nerve sparing technique aimed at preserving urinary control.

The surgeon demonstrates the position of the nerve, which is now moved away from the prostate gland on both sides, highlighting its role in innervating the entire ipsilateral pelvic floor, including the rectum. With the nerve out of the way, the surgeon prepares to begin the sexual function nerve sparing procedure.

The surgeon identifies the neurovascular bundles below the prostate gland and performs lateral dissection to clean the posterolateral corner. The dissection follows the plane from the base to the apex, revealing the pelvic floor contraction when the nerve is preserved. The surgeon carefully traces the nerve, ensuring minimal cutting to avoid capsulotomy, and confirms the preservation of the nerve with visible pelvic floor contraction.

The surgeon measures amplitude on the computer and uses the DaVinci Classic system to view the procedure on the monitor. The lateral area has been opened, and the surgeon proceeds medially, following a posterior plane with blunt dissection. The surgeon cuts where tissue is stuck and peels where it is easy, maneuvering around a flap of seminal vesicle. Two instruments are used to create space for clipping, ensuring clips go plastic on plastic to avoid issues with thicker tissue.

The surgeon traces the posterior plane around the prostate, cutting attachments where necessary and peeling tissue where possible. The posterior lateral corner is exposed, and the surgeon adjusts the positioning of the robotic arms for better access. The neurovascular bundles are separated, revealing a nerve bundle. The peroneal branch of the pudendal nerve is observed to be more lateral on this side compared to the other side, highlighting anatomical variability.

The surgeon uses a foot pedal to switch between bipolar cautery and nerve stimulation, ensuring no arcing occurs. The bipolar device is used to separate the prostate from the dorsal vein, with the jaws closing around the tissue to confirm the correct plane. Cautery is applied to maintain separation, avoiding the prostate gland due to its thickness.

The surgeon identifies the urethra and dorsal vein, noting anterior attachments. The fourth arm is used to retract back, revealing the neurovascular bundle on the side. Care is taken to minimize traction on the bundle. The surgeon addresses a small bleeder by cauterizing it, ensuring to avoid thermal spread to the neurovascular bundles and levator muscles. The perineal branch of the pudendal nerve is noted for its variable anatomical position relative to the neurovascular bundle.

The surgeon identifies the posterior edge of the prostate and proceeds to dissect through the rectourethralis. The perineal branch of the pudendal nerve is observed, with stimulation causing visible contraction of the rectum and levator muscles. The neurovascular bundle is visible, with the nerve located just beneath it.

The surgeon identifies a lip of tissue with longitudinal fibers and connects them to the rectourethralis using a PDS suture on an RB1 needle. Two sutures are tied together, running through the tissue twice to cinch it down securely.

The surgeon performs posterior reconstruction by attaching the visceral vesicle fascia to the rectourethralis using a single layer of sutures. Two instruments are used to rotate in opposite directions to pop the suture, while the fourth arm is utilized to keep the sutures organized and out of the way.

The surgeon begins suturing underneath a tissue layer to avoid placing a knot near the anastomosis. The bladder neck is identified, and a double throw technique is used to secure the knot, starting at the four o'clock position on the urethra and moving towards six o'clock. The posterior part of the bladder is brought into continuity with the posterior aspect of the urethra, without fixation, allowing the bladder's weight to rest on the anterior surface of the rectum and along the bed of the seminal vesicles.

The surgeon uses an RB-I needle to suture the bladder closer to the urethra, avoiding the Denonvilliers' fascia on the anterior surface of the rectum. The first suture bite is taken beyond the raw surface where the prostate was attached to the bladder, followed by a second bite at the mucosal level. This technique aims to restore the anatomical position of the fibers and conceal the raw surface area, replicating aspects of open surgery.
